system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
You are presented with 5 anonymous responses to the same brief. They are labeled E1 through E5.

**E1:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, **Option B is the right primary policy**, and the 3x infrastructure cost is the price of doing this category of product responsibly. **Why B wins on the axes that matter most:**

Health data is not ordinary product telemetry. It is among the most regulated, most sensitive, and most trust-contingent data a consumer hands over.

**E2:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, **Option B is the right primary policy**, despite its cost premium. **The decisive factor is trust under regulatory scrutiny.** Health data is among the most sensitive categories under HIPAA (US), GDPR Article 9 (EU), and emerging state laws (Washington's My Health My Data Act, etc.). A consumer health app sits in an awkward zone — often outside HIPAA's covered-entity perimeter but squarely inside consumer expectations of medical confidentiality.

**E3:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app serving 2M users, **Option B is the right primary policy**. Here's why it dominates on the axes that actually matter for this product. **Trust is the binding constraint, not cost.** Health data is among the most sensitive categories users hand over.

**E4:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, **Option B** is the right primary policy. Here's why I advocate for it over the alternatives. Health data is among the most sensitive personal information, and the trust posture of a health app is a core product asset, not a marketing surface.

**E5:** # Recommendation: Option D (Cloud with opt-in training only)

For a consumer health app at 2M MAU launching an AI coaching feature, **Option D is the right primary policy**. It is the only option that simultaneously preserves full product capability, keeps cost manageable, builds defensible trust, and stays clearly on the right side of regulators in every major jurisdiction. The decisive consideration is that health coaching quality matters morally, not just commercially.

Your task: for each of the following triads of 3 responses, identify a single bipolar construct that distinguishes them. Specifically, identify how TWO of the three are similar to each other in a way that distinguishes them from the THIRD. Name the two poles of that dimension.

Triads to analyze:
Triad 1: E2, E3, E5
Triad 2: E1, E3, E5
Triad 3: E1, E2, E5

For each triad, output EXACTLY this format:
Triad N:
Left pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>
Right pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>

Be specific. Avoid generic dichotomies like 'good vs bad'.
raw_response_content:
Triad 1 (E2, E3, E5):
Left pole: Prioritizes regulatory/trust constraints as binding (E2, E3)
Right pole: Prioritizes product capability and coaching quality (E5)

Triad 2 (E1, E3, E5):
Left pole: Accepts cost premium for privacy posture (E1, E3)
Right pole: Treats cost manageability as decisive criterion (E5)

Triad 3 (E1, E2, E5):
Left pole: Rejects any use of data for training (E1, E2)
Right pole: Permits opt-in training to improve coaching (E5)

message.content:
Triad 1 (E2, E3, E5):
Left pole: Prioritizes regulatory/trust constraints as binding (E2, E3)
Right pole: Prioritizes product capability and coaching quality (E5)

Triad 2 (E1, E3, E5):
Left pole: Accepts cost premium for privacy posture (E1, E3)
Right pole: Treats cost manageability as decisive criterion (E5)

Triad 3 (E1, E2, E5):
Left pole: Rejects any use of data for training (E1, E2)
Right pole: Permits opt-in training to improve coaching (E5)
